stem: Use Green's theorem to find the area of the region \( R \) enclosed by the lines \( x = 0 \), \( x = [[W]] \), \( y = 0 \), and \( y = [[m]]x + [[k]] \).
solution: By Green's theorem the area equals \( \iint_R 1\,dA \). Integrating over \( 0\le x\le [[W]] \), \( 0\le y\le [[m]]x+[[k]] \) gives \( \int_0^{[[W]]} ([[m]]x+[[k]])\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( [[m]][[W]]^{2} \) (no factor of \( \tfrac12 \)) for the triangular part.
This counts only the rectangular part \( [[k]]\cdot[[W]] \), dropping the triangular part.
This counts only the triangular part, dropping the rectangular part \( [[k]]\cdot[[W]] \).
This evaluates the top boundary height at \( x=[[W]] \) instead of integrating for the area.
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by one from the correct area.

stem: Green's theorem gives the area enclosed by a simple closed curve \( C \) as \( A = \tfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\oint_C (x\,dy - y\,dx) \). Use this boundary formula to find the area enclosed by the ellipse \( \mathbf{r}(t) = (([[a]])\cos t,\; ([[b]])\sin t) \), \( 0 \le t \le 2\pi \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the (incorrect) formula \( \pi(a+b) \) for an ellipse's area instead of \( \pi a b \).
This doubles the correct area, forgetting the \( \tfrac12 \) in the boundary formula.
This uses only the semi-axis \( a \), as if the curve were a circle of radius \( a \).
This uses only the semi-axis \( b \), as if the curve were a circle of radius \( b \).
This adds a spurious \( \pi \) to the correct area.

stem: Use Green's theorem to find the area of the region enclosed between the \( x \)-axis and the parabolic arch \( y = ([[m]])x([[W]]-x) \) for \( 0 \le x \le [[W]] \).
solution: By Green's theorem the area equals \( \iint_R 1\,dA = \int_0^{[[W]]} ([[m]])x([[W]]-x)\,dx \). Expanding and integrating the quadratic boundary gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the area formula for a TRIANGLE of the same base and peak height instead of integrating the actual parabola.
This drops a factor of 2 from the correct coefficient of the parabolic-arch area formula.
This only accounts for a linear (triangular) boundary, not the quadratic arch.
This is off by one from the correct area.
This is off by one from the correct area.
